competitors.label: State as a Snapshot
competitors.selectionReason: This is the closest canonical React page for explaining render snapshots and why event handlers see state from a particular render.
competitors.winReason: The official docs teach render snapshots clearly, but our page is more directly centered on stale-closure bugs in timers, async handlers, subscriptions, and the specific fixes interviewers ask for next.
theirStrengths: It is the official baseline for the render-snapshot mental model behind stale closures.
theirGaps: It is less explicitly bug-focused on intervals, async effects, and fix patterns.

realWorldUseCases: Stale state in React closures is really a render-snapshot bug: each render creates new closures, and async code may keep reading an older one.
followUpConfusion: The fix is not React is wrong; it is understanding when to use functional updates, refs, or correct dependencies.

realWorldUseCases: The docs explain snapshot semantics well, but they are less centered on production bugs like intervals, delayed submits, and long-lived async handlers.
actionableExamples: They include state snapshot examples, so the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: Our page is stronger on the likely follow-up: which fix to choose between dependency updates, refs, and functional state updates.

actionableExamples: Add one explicit async submit example alongside the timer example.

competitors.label: React Hooks for Interviews
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's hooks playbook is directly relevant because stale closures usually surface in hook, dependency, and effect discussions.
competitors.winReason: The hooks playbook is relevant, but our page is more explicitly organized around stale-closure incidents and the three practical fixes: dependencies, functional updates, and refs.
theirStrengths: It is interview-oriented and covers hook pitfalls that lead into stale closure questions.
theirGaps: It is broader and less concentrated on stale closures than a dedicated page.

realWorldUseCases: That is why intervals, delayed handlers, and effects sometimes log or submit outdated values.
followUpConfusion: If you are updating state based on previous state, don’t read from the closure at all.

realWorldUseCases: The playbook covers hook mistakes broadly, but our page is more focused on concrete stale-value incidents.
actionableExamples: Both pages provide hook guidance and practical fixes, so the example criterion remains close.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is more direct about choosing between functional updates, refs, and dependency corrections.

actionableExamples: Add one subscription callback example that reads a stale prop or state value.

competitors.label: How React Uses Closures to Avoid Bugs
competitors.selectionReason: EpicReact has a strong dedicated article on closures, async timing, and why function components preserve the render-time snapshot.
theirStrengths: It gives a detailed async bug walkthrough and shows exactly how closures preserve render-time values.
theirGaps: It is a long-form article rather than a compact interview answer.

realWorldUseCases: Worked async example: delayed submit + permission flow
actionableExamples: Keep the latest permission in a ref for long-lived async work

realWorldUseCases: The article centers on an async stale-value bug and compares class and hook behavior in detail.
actionableExamples: It contains more worked code examples and async timing walkthroughs than our current page.
followUpConfusion: Both pages explain render snapshots and why async code can see older values, so this criterion remains close.

realWorldUseCases: Add one richer async example with a delayed submit or permission check, not only interval-style examples.
actionableExamples: Add a before-and-after example using refs to avoid stale callbacks.
followUpConfusion: Keep one extra one-liner that contrasts intentional snapshot behavior with true stale-state bugs.

competitors.winReason: Our page now covers a delayed-submit permission flow and a ref-based latest-state repair in a compact interview format, while the broader closure follow-up remains close.
